name: Congo Crisis
summary: The Congo Crisis was a cluster of overlapping post-independence conflicts in Congo-Léopoldville/DRC, including the ANC mutiny, Belgian intervention, Katanga and South Kasai secessions, ONUC operations, and the Kwilu/Simba rebellions. The conventional death toll is about 100,000, but some sub-conflict surveys imply a much higher upper bound if their ranges are summed. Most deaths were civilians killed in massacres, repression, hostage executions, and famine/displacement, and many figures cannot be cleanly attributed to one side.

sides.name: Congolese central government and pro-government forces
members: Republic of the Congo / Democratic Republic of the Congo government

military_killed.notes: Documented minimum from PRIO/Clodfelter for the 1964 anti-Simba campaign: about 200 Congolese soldiers and 35 mercenaries killed. Total government-side losses across all phases are not cleanly reported.

breakdown.notes: About 3,000 Baluba civilians killed is commonly cited for the ANC invasion; Sciences Po gives 5,000-10,000 civilian deaths in South Kasai clashes involving ANC and pro-Kalonji militias. Attribution between ANC, Katangese, and local militias is not fully separable.
Kwilu rebellion sources report over 3,000 civilians killed in one day at Kikwit by ANC/mercenary forces; broader ANC pacification totals are highly contested.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The direct-civilian low combines the best documented South Kasai and Kikwit minimums. A much higher figure is possible, but higher Kwilu repression figures conflict with other sub-conflict totals.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Displacement after ethnic conflict and the ANC offensive contributed to famine and epidemic malnutrition; sources report at least 200 refugees dying per day in December 1960, but no reliable cumulative total.
ANC and mercenary counterinsurgency reportedly razed villages and destroyed harvests; indirect deaths from hunger and disease are not separable from direct killings in available sources.

sides.name: Katanga and South Kasai secessionist forces

military_killed.notes: Documented Katangese/secessionist losses include 202 killed in Operation Unokat and 50+ in Operation Grandslam. PRIO/Clodfelter gives 503 battle deaths in Katanga in 1961 and 80 in 1962, but those are conflict totals rather than clean Katanga-only losses.

breakdown.victims_group: Patrice Lumumba, Maurice Mpolo, Joseph Okito
breakdown.notes: The three prisoners were killed after transfer to Katanga on 17 January 1961; Katangese personnel and Belgian-linked officers/mercenaries were involved.

Katangese forces participated in violence against Baluba/BALUBAKAT populations, but available figures such as about 3,000 killed or 5,000-10,000 South Kasai civilians killed are not cleanly attributable between ANC, Katangese, and local forces.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Only the Lumumba/Mpolo/Okito execution is a cleanly attributable minimum. Larger Baluba civilian deaths are known but not assignable solely to secessionist forces.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Katangese/local violence contributed to Baluba displacement and famine; sources report 35,000 refugees in Elisabethville camps and as many as 100,000 seeking refuge in Bakwanga, but no secessionist-only indirect death total.

sides.name: Lumumbist / CNL / Simba and Kwilu rebels
members: Free Republic of the Congo at Stanleyville

military_killed.notes: PRIO/Clodfelter reports 2,000 or more Simba killed in action and another 2,000 executed. Kwilu and other rebel military deaths are not cleanly tabulated.

breakdown.victims_group: foreign hostages at Stanleyville and other Simba-held areas
breakdown.notes: 24 hostages killed during Operation Dragon Rouge and 185 foreign hostages left behind executed by mid-December 1964.

breakdown.victims_group: Westernized Congolese and perceived collaborators in Stanleyville
breakdown.notes: PRIO/Clodfelter notes about 1,000-2,000 Westernized Congolese murdered in Stanleyville alone.

breakdown.victims_group: Black Congolese civilians in eastern rebellion zones
breakdown.notes: PRIO/Clodfelter reports at least 18,000 Black civilians murdered by the two sides, so these deaths cannot be assigned solely to the rebels.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Low is a conservative documented minimum from foreign-hostage and Stanleyville political killings. Larger civilian totals are partly two-sided or overlap with government repression.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: civilians in rebel-held Kwilu and eastern Congo
deaths_from_actions.notes: Sources describe hunger, disease, and displacement as rebel-held areas collapsed, but indirect deaths caused by rebel governance cannot be separated from the effects of government counterinsurgency.

sides.name: United Nations Operation in the Congo (ONUC)

military_killed.notes: Official UN mission page lists 245 military personnel killed/died, plus 5 international civilian staff, for 250 ONUC fatalities.

breakdown.notes: Reported during Operation Grandslam; broader ONUC-caused civilian casualties are not systematically tabulated in sources found.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: No reliable aggregate civilian death toll caused directly by ONUC fire was found.

military_killed.notes: Operation Dragon Rouge lists 2 Belgian paratroopers killed and 12 wounded. Total Belgian losses across all regular/covert/adviser categories are not cleanly reported.

breakdown.victims_group: Patrice Lumumba, Maurice Mpolo, Joseph Okito
breakdown.notes: Belgian officials/officers/mercenaries were implicated in the transfer, torture context, and execution environment, but direct killing is generally attributed to Katangese separatists/police/gendarmes; counted under Katanga above to avoid double counting.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: No quantified civilian deaths directly caused by Belgian/U.S. intervention forces as distinct from allied Congolese/Katangese forces were found.

totals.notes: Grand low is the conventional about-100,000 war-related deaths estimate. Grand high is a broad, non-audited upper bound derived from summing Kisangani's 1960-65/overlapping sub-conflict ranges; it may double count and partly extends into 1966. Military low sums only documented minimums: 235 government/mercenary, 252 secessionist, 4,000 Simba, 245 ONUC, and 2 Belgian; real military deaths were higher but not cleanly known.

key_atrocities.name: South Kasai / Baluba massacres and famine
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Congolese central government / ANC, with local and Katangese violence also implicated
key_atrocities.notes: About 3,000 civilians killed is common for ANC atrocities; Sciences Po gives 5,000-10,000 civilian deaths in clashes. Famine among refugees caused at least 200 deaths per day in December 1960 but no cumulative total is known.

key_atrocities.name: Execution of Lumumba, Mpolo, and Okito
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Katanga secessionist authorities with Belgian-linked involvement
key_atrocities.notes: The three were killed after transfer to Katanga on 17 January 1961.

key_atrocities.name: Kindu massacre
key_atrocities.notes: Thirteen Italian UN airmen were killed by Congolese soldiers at Kindu.

key_atrocities.name: Stanleyville hostage killings / Operation Dragon Rouge
key_atrocities.notes: 24 hostages were killed during the rescue operation; by mid-December 185 foreign hostages left behind, plus several thousand Congolese civilians, had reportedly been executed.

key_atrocities.name: Kikwit / Kwilu counterinsurgency massacre
key_atrocities.notes: Sources describe over 3,000 civilians killed in one day at Kikwit during government counterinsurgency.

uncertainty_notes: The Congo Crisis casualty literature is unusually fragmented. Many figures are by episode or province rather than perpetrator; several large figures are explicitly two-sided; and battle-death, massacre, displacement, and famine deaths are often mixed. The conventional total of about 100,000 is best treated as a conservative war-related estimate, while the higher range reflects sub-conflict surveys that may overlap and are not a clean rollup.
